perturbation.original_text:
3.1 Seller's Agreement. (a) Seller agrees that during the Non-
------------------ Competition Period Seller will not, and will not permit any of its Subsidiaries to: (i) engage in the Product Business; (ii) encourage any Service Account to specify a party other than CompuCom to provide Products and image load/configurations to such Service Account; or (iii) solicit, entice or induce any employee of CompuCom or any Subsidiary of CompuCom to terminate his or her employment with CompuCom or any Subsidiary of CompuCom or hire any person who was or is at any time from the date of execution of the Asset Purchase Agreement to the end of the Non-Competition Period an employee of CompuCom or any Subsidiary of CompuCom.
perturbation.changed_text:
3.1 Seller's Agreement. (a) Seller agrees that during the Non-
------------------ Competition Period Seller will not, and will not permit any of its Subsidiaries to: (i) engage in the Product Business, except in situations where such engagement would be deemed beneficial to the subsidiary's overall financial performance; (ii) encourage any Service Account to specify a party other than CompuCom to provide Products and image load/configurations to such Service Account, unless the Service Account explicitly requests an alternative provider; or (iii) solicit, entice or induce any employee of CompuCom or any Subsidiary of CompuCom to terminate his or her employment with CompuCom or any Subsidiary of CompuCom or hire any person who was or is at any time from the date of execution of the Asset Purchase Agreement to the end of the Non-Competition Period an employee of CompuCom or any Subsidiary of CompuCom, provided that such hiring does not exceed 5 employees per year.
perturbation.explanation: The additions of 'except in situations where such engagement would be deemed beneficial to the subsidiary's overall financial performance,' 'unless the Service Account explicitly requests an alternative provider,' and 'provided that such hiring does not exceed 5 employees per year' create ambiguities in the non-compete agreement. The terms 'deemed beneficial', 'explicitly requests', and the numerical limit lack clear definitions and could be interpreted in multiple ways, making the obligations difficult to enforce.
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware law grants corporations broad powers to manage their business affairs. By introducing subjective exceptions ('deemed beneficial', 'explicitly requests') to the non-compete clause, the modification could allow Seller to argue that certain actions, even if technically competitive, fall within these exceptions, thus undermining the enforceability of the non-compete agreement and contradicting the intent of ensuring fair competition and protecting the value of the acquired assets.

General Corporation Law Subchapter IV. Directors and Officers Â§ 141. Board of directors; powers; number, qualifications, terms and quorum; committees; classes of directors; nonstock corporations; reliance upon books; action without meeting; removal. (a) The business and affairs of every corporation organized under this chapter shall be managed by or under the direction of a board of directors, except as may be otherwise provided in this chapter or in its certificate of incorporation. If any such provision is made in the certificate of incorporation, the powers and duties conferred or imposed upon the board of directors by this chapter shall be exercised or performed to such extent and by such person or persons as shall be provided in the certificate of incorporation. (b) The board of directors of a corporation shall consist of 1 or more members, each of whom shall be a natural person. The number of directors shall be fixed by, or in the manner provided in, the bylaws, unless the certificate of incorporation fixes the number of directors, in which case a change in the number of directors shall be made only by amendment of the certificate. Directors need not be stockholders unless so required by the certificate of incorporation or the bylaws. The certificate of incorporation or bylaws may prescribe other qualifications for directors. Each director shall hold office until such directorâs successor is elected and qualified or until such directorâs earlier resignation or removal. Any director may resign at any time upon notice given in writing or by electronic transmission to the corporation. A resignation is effective when the resignation is delivered unless the resignation specifies a later effective date or an effective date determined upon the happening of an event or events. A resignation which is conditioned upon the director failing to receive a spe
perturbation.justification: The added exceptions to the non-compete clause introduce ambiguities that undermine its enforceability. While Delaware law, as suggested by the Law Explanation, grants corporations broad powers (and snippet 1 references directors managing business affairs), the specific modifications introduce terms like 'deemed beneficial' and 'explicitly requests' which lack clear definitions. This ambiguity can allow the seller to argue that certain competitive actions fall within these exceptions, weakening the non-compete agreement's intent to ensure fair competition.

perturbation.original_text: CompuCom agrees that during the Non-Competition Period, it will accept orders for Products submitted to CompuCom by Seller pursuant to Section 3.1(b) under the following conditions: (i) CompuCom has the ability to offer such Product for sale; (ii) the purchase price required by the customer order for the Product is reasonably acceptable to CompuCom; (iii) such Product offered by CompuCom meets the customer's specifications, including, but not limited to, configuration specifications; and (iv) the requirements of the customer with respect to such Product, including but not limited to delivery, payment terms and return privileges are reasonably acceptable to CompuCom.
perturbation.changed_text: CompuCom agrees that during the Non-Competition Period, it will try to accept orders for Products submitted to CompuCom by Seller pursuant to Section 3.1(b) under the following conditions: (i) CompuCom has the ability to offer such Product for sale; (ii) the purchase price required by the customer order for the Product is somewhat acceptable to CompuCom; (iii) such Product offered by CompuCom generally meets the customer's specifications, including, but not limited to, configuration specifications; and (iv) the requirements of the customer with respect to such Product, including but not limited to delivery, payment terms and return privileges are potentially acceptable to CompuCom.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will accept' to 'will try to accept' and introducing qualifiers like 'somewhat acceptable,' 'generally meets,' and 'potentially acceptable' introduces significant ambiguity. This weakens CompuCom's obligation to accept orders, allowing for subjective interpretation and potential disputes. The original text implied a more definite commitment, while the modified text provides loopholes for CompuCom to reject orders based on vague criteria.
perturbation.law_explanation: While § 2-305 primarily deals with open price terms, the principle of good faith and commercial reasonableness applies to all aspects of a contract under the UCC. By making CompuCom's acceptance of orders contingent on vague and subjective criteria, the modification creates an opportunity for CompuCom to act in bad faith by arbitrarily rejecting orders, thereby violating the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing in contract performance.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity that undermines the original obligation. The original text states CompuCom 'will accept' orders, creating a clear obligation. The changed text uses phrases like 'try to accept,' 'somewhat acceptable,' 'generally meets,' and 'potentially acceptable,' significantly weakening this obligation and introducing subjective criteria. This contradicts the principle of good faith and fair dealing, as the UCC implies. Snippet 1, § 2-301, states 'The obligation of the seller is to transfer and deliver and that of the buyer is to accept and pay in accordance with the contract.' By making acceptance contingent on vague criteria, CompuCom could arbitrarily reject orders, acting in bad faith and potentially violating the implied duty of good faith. The changes introduce ambiguity, making the contract terms less enforceable and more open to dispute, directly contradicting the defined parameters for Ambiguity Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text:
5.1 Seller's Non-Disclosure Obligations. Seller covenants and agrees
----------------------------------- that, unless otherwise required by law, from and after the Closing:
(a) Seller shall cooperate with CompuCom at CompuCom's expense to protect and safeguard all of CompuCom's Confidential Information; and
(b) Seller shall not, directly or indirectly, use, sell, license, publish, disclose, or otherwise transfer or make available to others any of CompuCom's Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text:
5.1 Seller's Non-Disclosure Obligations. Seller covenants and agrees
----------------------------------- that, unless absolutely required by law, from and after the Closing:
(a) Seller should cooperate with CompuCom at CompuCom's expense to generally protect and safeguard all of CompuCom's Confidential Information; and
(b) Seller shall not, only when absolutely necessary, directly or indirectly, use, sell, license, publish, disclose, or otherwise transfer or make available to others any of CompuCom's Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall cooperate' to 'should cooperate', inserting 'generally protect', and adding 'only when absolutely necessary' introduce significant ambiguity and weaken Seller's non-disclosure obligations. The phrases 'absolutely required by law', 'should cooperate', 'generally protect', and 'only when absolutely necessary' are open to subjective interpretation. This reduces the enforceability of the non-disclosure agreement, as Seller could argue that certain disclosures were 'generally' protected or 'absolutely necessary' under vague circumstances.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Delaware Trade Secrets Act (DTSA) aims to protect confidential business information. By weakening the non-disclosure obligations and introducing subjective qualifiers, the modification undermines the protection of CompuCom's trade secrets. The DTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, and the modified language provides loopholes that could be used to argue that Seller's actions, even if they resulted in disclosure, were not in violation of the agreement because they were 'generally protected' or 'absolutely necessary,' contradicting the intent of the DTSA.

Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or C. Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or 3. Before a material change of the personâs position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not b
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces ambiguity and weakens the seller's non-disclosure obligations, potentially undermining the protection of CompuCom's confidential information. The original contract had clear obligations ('shall cooperate', 'shall not'), whereas the modified text introduces subjective qualifiers such as 'absolutely required by law', 'should cooperate', 'generally protect', and 'only when absolutely necessary'. According to Scraped Law Snippet 1, section 2001, improper means includes 'breach of a duty to maintain secrecy'. The changes weaken the duty to maintain secrecy by making it subjective, opening the door for potential misappropriation of trade secrets. The modified text creates loopholes that could be used to argue that Seller's actions, even if they resulted in disclosure, were not in violation of the agreement because they were 'generally protected' or 'absolutely necessary,' contradicting the intent of maintaining secrecy. This falls under the definition of Ambiguity Legal Contradiction.
